1   Installation of the master module


Installation of the MASTER:

                                                                           Do not mix up the connectors! This will destroy
             Controller connector                                          your electronics. Do not force in cables in the
             The connector for the controllers has 6 pins (two             wrong orientiation or with the wrong number of
             rows of three pins) and is used for connecting a              pins! Do not attach anything to the program-
             chain of B32, P2B8, P4B2, B32, P10, P8S8 and                  ming port.
             M4. Also the X7 is connected here. An X7 must
             always be the ﬁrst in the chain.


             Programming port
             The 6 pin programming port is not mounted in a
             box. Caution: Do not connect anything to this
             port! It is solely for the initial programming in our
             labs. Later ﬁrmware upgrades are done via the               Expansion port for G8 expanders
             Micro SD card.
                                                                         The connector for the G8 expanders has 8 pins
                                                                         (two rows of four pins). Here you can add up to
                                                                         four G8 expanders for an additional 8 - 32 gate in-
                                                                         puts/outputs. Please refer to page 75 for details.




             Power connector
               The power connector has 10 pins (two rows of
               ﬁve pins). Use the shipped 10 pin ribbon ca-
               ble in order to connect it with the bus board of
               your Eurorack case. Important: Put the red
               stripe down!

                                                                         Diagnostic LEDs
                                                                         The MASTER18 does not have LEDs on the front
                                                                         panel. Instead diagnostic information is displayed
                                                                         with these four LEDs on the back of the module.
                                                                         Usually you don’t need this information. If you
             Expansion port for G8 expanders                             run in trouble you can unscrew the module and
                                                                         get some additional information of what’s going

             four G8 expanders for an additional 8 - 32 gate in-
             puts/outputs. Please refer to page 75 for details.

2   Getting started

2.1 DROID explained


       is ﬂexible system for generating and processing     The MASTER has                                             2.2   Creating DROID patches
control voltages in your Eurorack modular system. It
can do almost any CV task you can imagine, including           • 8 CV inputs with high quality 16 bit converters
sequencing, melody generation, quantizing, switching,          • 8 CV outputs with high quality 16 bit converters     To bring your        system to life, you need to create a
mixing, working on clocks and triggers, envelopes, LFOs,       • a 4 × 4 multicolor LED matrix displaying the state   Droid patch and load it to your master.
random voltages and any combination of these at the              of the inputs and outputs.
                                                                                                                      What is a Droid patch? Well, the          is like a self con-
same time. It also give ﬂexible access to MIDI.
                                                           The MASTER18 has                                           tained modular system for CV in a module. In order to
The base of every         system is a DROID master mod-                                                               avoid confusion with “real” modules – the building blocks
ule. There are two kinds to choose from:                       • 8 CV outputs with high quality 16 bit converters     in a Droid patch are called circuits. There are very sim-
                                                               • 2 inputs and 4 outputs for gates and triggers        ple circuits like a mixer for CVs. And there are also very
                                                               • A builtin VCO tuning device                          complex circuits like an sophisticated algorithmic trigger
                                                               • a USB-C connector for MIDI and fast conﬁguration     sequencer called algoquencer (see page 115).
                                                               • 2 MIDI inputs via TRS (3.5 mm jacks)
                                                               • 2 MIDI outputs via TRS (3.5 mm jacks)                Much like real modules, the circuits have input and out-
                                                                                                                      put jacks. These are called inputs or outputs, or some-
                                                           Extension modules:                                         times also “parameters”. Each of them can be set to a
                                                                                                                      ﬁxed value, wired to one of            ’s physical inputs or
                                                               • You can add USB and MIDI to your MASTER by           ouputs, set by a knob or button on a Droid controller or
                                                                 adding an X7 expander (see page 77).                 internally wired to other circuits in order to create more
                                                               • You can add up to 32 additional gate inputs and      complex patches.
                                                                 outputs to your master by adding up to four G8 ex-
                                                                 panders (see page 75).                               A Droid patch lists all the circuits you want to use and de-
                                                               • And ﬁnally you can add up to 16 controllers with     scribes how they are connected and how the parameters
                                                                 potentiometer, buttons, encoders, motor faders,      are set.
                                                                 switches and more to your master to complete the
                                                                 system.                                              Technically, a patch is a small text ﬁle with the name
                                                                                                                      droid.ini, which is located on the micro SD card in the
                                                           Note: In this manual whenever I write “master” in lower    SD slot of the master. You can create and modify this
                                                           case I refer to both MASTER and MASTER18.                  ﬁle with any text editor you like, and the chapter Writing
                                                                                                                      Droid patches with a text editor goes in all length through
                                                                                                                      the structure of that ﬁle (see page 47).

                                                                                                                      plication for Mac and Windows called the Droid Forge

A ﬁrst patch example – step by step


So let’s start! First install the Droid Forge. Download it    Now let’s create a ﬁrst simple patch. The ﬁrst step is to
from the upper link and install it to your Windows PC or      choose wether to use MASTER or MASTER18. Select this
Mac. After starting it you get a window like in the screen-   in the menu Rack in the entry Master module. For this ex-
shot above. The Window is divided into three areas:           ample we assume the MASTER.

                                                              Then it’s time to add your ﬁrst circuit. From the Edit menu
                                                              choose New circuit.... This opens a dialog for adding a cir-
                                                              cuit to your patch:




                                                                                                                             Input are written in blue, outputs in red. You learn about
                                                                                                                             all available parameters of a circuit in its chapter here in
                                                                                                                             this manual. Have a look at the LFO circuit on page 239.
                                                                                                                             For example:

                                                                                                                                 • hz sets the speed of the LFO in cycles per second.

Inputs and ouptuts                                                                                                           You might for example have an internal clock in your
                                                                                                                             patch (created with an LFO circuit) that can be overrid-
The MASTER has eight CV inputs and eight CV ouputs,                                                                          den by patching something into I1.
both ranging from -10 V to +10 V. The inputs are abbre-
                                                                                                                             To do that, send your internal LFO clock signal to N1. Then
viated with I1, I2, ... I8, the outputs with O1, O2, ... O8.
                                                                                                                             let the rest of the patch use I1 as clock input.
The MASTER18 does not have CV inputs, but instead it
has two trigger/gate inputs called I1 and I2 and four trig-
ger/gate outputs called G1 .. G4.

lenging, especially if you are just at the beginning of your       • Should the output be via MIDI or CV/Gate?              make in a dialog in the Forge. For example in this dialog
journey. So what people have suggested since the begin-            • What parameters per step should there be?              you would select the number of tracks, the conﬁguration
ning was a good collection of ready-to-use patches.                • Shall we use two our four M4 controllers?              of modules to use and so on. Then you press OK and get
                                                                   • Should it rely on a G8 expander for gate output?       a patch that you can upload to your master. Since it’s a
While this idea sounds appealing, it’s actually not as easy        • What type of master module should it use?              normal patch, you can edit it before this. For example
as it seems. Everybody has as a different set of modules           • ... and so on.                                         you could rearrange the buttons by dragging and drop-
and controllers, and different ideas. And creating an vari-                                                                 ping them with the mouse.
ant for every possible situation would vastly multiply the     It’s obvious that creating one dedicated patch for each
number of needed patches.                                      variant like one for “Sequencer for three tracks with two    As a start the Forge comes with one ﬁrst patch generator:

sequencer based on M4 motor fader controllers (see page                                                                     You can even create your own patch generators. But
71). There are so many possible conﬁgrations:                  So we had to ﬁnd a better solution. And here it is: the      that’s a topic that will be covered in a future version of

4.2 Enable the patch generators


The patch generators are not enabled by default. The rea-      Python to environment variables”, make sure that you en-
son is that additional software needs to be installed on       able it. Otherwise the Forge won’t be able to ﬁnd the
your box. This software is the programming language            Python interpreter and thinks it’s not installed.
“Python 3”. It’s very common but still may be missing on
your system.                                                   To enable the patch generators select the menu File /
                                                               Patch generators / Enable patch generators.. This will
If you are running the Forge on Windows, you should now        bring up the following popup:
install Python 3, if it’s not already there. You can do this
either from the Microsoft store. The exact version (3.11,
3.12 or whatever) is not important. It just needs to be
version 3. Or you can get Python directly from its ofﬁcial
home page. This is at Python downloads for Windows.

                                                                     cently used patch generator. So redoing the gener-

Introduction                                                     • up to 32 steps per track                              parameters. You can switch to the arpeggiator with a but-

                                                                 • performance menu with toplevel control over all       Call the patch generator Motor Fader Performance Se-

                                                                 • Get root, scale and transposition from a Sinfonion    An easy way to thoroughly learn the sequencer is start-
                                                                 • Mini arpeggiator for repeats and ratchets             ing with the preset Minimum (1 track) and play with the
                                                                 • Pitch accumulator                                     sequencer for a few minutes. Than add more and more
                                                                 • and much more...                                      features as you like.
This patch generator creates a patch for a performance
sequencer based on M4 motor fader controllers (see page      To use this sequencer, you need the following modules:      As an alternative you can load the default preset and start
71). The sequencer aims at creating interesting melodies                                                                 with the whole features set with four tracks and three
for bass lines and lead voices. It is not so much about          • 1× MASTER or MASTER18                                 presets per track. This is also the conﬁguration the Moto
drum sequencing, even if you could use it for that task,         • 1× P2B8                                               Kit comes preloaded with. If you have purchased that,
as well.                                                         • 1× B32                                                you got a sheet of stickers. We suggest using these stick-

The Motor Fader Performance Sequencer (MFPS) excels                                                                      satisﬁed with your feature set.
in situations where you are performing. If offers lots of    You can extend the patch by adding further modules:
features that are useful in live improvisation, ﬁnding in-                                                               If you like, you can reorder some of the buttons with the
                                                                 • up to three G8s provide more gate ouptuts
spiring melodies and simply and playing music. Especially                                                                mouse via drag & drop before you ﬁnally label them.
                                                                 • an X7 adds MIDI support to your MASTER
the fast and intuitive control with the M4 faders with
                                                                 • two additional M4s let you edit 16 steps without      And now let’s see, how this sequencer works...
force feedback make it stand out amongst all existing Eu-
                                                                   switching
rorack sequencers.
                                                             The sequencer has one to eight tracks. Each track con-
Here are some of the features:
                                                             trols one external instrument, either via CV/Gate or via
    • up to 8 parallel tracks                                MIDI. When using MIDI, you can assign multiple tracks to
    • output via CV/gate and/or MIDI                         the same channel and thus create polyphony.
    • builtin arpeggiator per track
                                                             Each track consists of a normal melody sequencer with
    • up to four presets for each track
                                                             8, 16 or 32 steps. In addition it has a builtin arpeggia-
    • gatelength, velocity and glide per step
                                                             tor, which creates alogrithmic melodies based on many
    • pitch and gate randomization

notches (force feedback). Each notch represents one of
                                                               means that you have either 8 or 16 faders. You set this
the selected scale notes. If you alter the note selection by
                                                               in the tab Modules.
switching on and off intervals on you P2B8, the number of

Touching the buttons below the faders toggle the gates
for the steps.




                                                                                                                                    In the default conﬁguration there are four tracks,
                                                                                                                                    each has 16 steps. With two M4’s you can control
                                                                                                                             eight steps at a time. The button PAGE switches to edit-
                                                                                                                             ing steps 9 - 16 while it is lit.

                                                                                                                             track does the same as the PAGE button: it toggles the
                                                               Independent of this you can set the length of the tracks      current page.

                                                                                                                             tons for paging, labelled 1 - 8, 9 - 16, 17 - 24 and 25 - 31.

                                                                                                                             tween tracks). Press the COPY button to copy the con-
                                                                                                                             tents of the current page into an internal clipboard.

The MIDI channels for the individual tracks are set in the   might want to change this.                                    The Output for clock with user defined divider sends the
tab MIDI:                                                                                                                  sequencer clock to the outside, but with a division from

                                                             Connectivity                                                  menu from bottom (1) to top (16).

                                                                                                                           If you enable Sinfonion link, you can have the sequencer
                                                                                                                           follow the musical state of a Sinfonion. This requires a
                                                                                                                           MASTER18. To do this:

                                                                                                                              1. Set OUT 1 of the Sinfonion to Sync master.
                                                                                                                              2. Draw a standard patch cable from OUT 1 of the Sin-
                                                                                                                                 fonion to I1 of your MASTER18.
                                                                                                                              3. Enable Sinfonion link in the patch generator.

                                                                                                                           If you do this, the current root note, scale and transpo-
                                                                                                                           sition of the Sinfonion is automatically used by the se-
                                                                                                                           quencer. Beware: while you do this, you should keep the
                                                                                                                           corresponding three faders of the tonality menu in their
                                                                                                                           neutral position, because they will add up. For example if
                                                                                                                           you set root to C♯ in your sequencer, your root note will
                                                                                                                           be always one semitone off. You need to set it to C (fader
                                                                                                                           completely down).

4.5 Droid Megasequencer


Overview                                                       • Instant hands-on creation of melodies and chords.      to the start.
                                                               • Polyphony with up to 16 voices in parallel for two,
                                                                 three or four instruments.
                                                               • Independent clock divisions, pattern length and        Outputs:
                                                                 lots for more features for creative musical jour-
                                                                 neys.                                                  G9: Clock output
                                                               • MIDI output via USB and DIN/TRS.
                                                                                                                        All the other outputs and inputs are free. You can attach
                                                                                                                        functions to it by adapting the Droid patch.

                                                           Modules

5.2 Basic structure of the patch ﬁle


Droid offeres a long list of pre-programmed functionali-       the inputs to values you like. Such a value could be taken   circuits in order to create more complex patches.
ties - called circuits - from which you can pick and choose    from a hardware input, a button, a pot, or simply be a
for your needs. Each circuit takes input values, processes     ﬁxed value. The outputs of the circuit can be connected      All this is conﬁgured in a simple text ﬁle with the name
them and produces output values. It is your task to set        to hardware outputs, LEDs or even to the inputs of other     droid.ini, which is also called the Droid patch. Using

DROID manual for blue-6                                                                 51                                                               Table of contents at page 2
Registers on the MASTER:

 Register                                  Type            Description

 I1   I2    I3   I4   I5   I6   I7   I8    input           The eight CV inputs

 N1   N2    N3   N4   N5   N6   N7   N8    output          The normalization of these inputs. When nothing is patched into an input, the according I-register will take its value
                                                           from the matching N- register instead. Any they are 0.0 if you have not set them.

 O1   O2    O3   O4   O5   O6   O7   O8    output          The eight CV outputs

 G1   G2    G3   G4   G5   G6   G7   G8    input/output    The jacks of the ﬁrst G8 expander. Each can be used either as an input or as an output. Instead of G1 you can write G1.1.

 G2.1 G2.2 G2.3 G2.4        ...   G2.8     input/output    The eight gate jacks of the second G8 expander. Use G3.X and G4.X for the third and fourth G8 expander.

 G9 G10 G11 G12                            output          The four gate jacks of the X7 expander. These are always outputs.

 R1   R2    R3   R4   R5   R6   R7   R8    output          The colored LED squares in the ﬁrst two rows (those for the inputs)

 R9   R10 R11 R12 R13 R14 R15 R16          output          The colored LED squares in row three and four (those for the outputs)

 R17 ... R48                               output          The colored LED squares on the ﬁrst, second, third and fourth G8 expander

 R49 ... R56                               output          The colored LED squares on the X7 expander

 X1                                        output          Special register for displaying a value encoded in the 4 × 4 LED matrix

Registers on the MASTER18:

 Register                                 Type            Description

 I1   I2                                  input           Gate/trigger inputs

 O1   O2    O3   O4   O5   O6   O7   O8   output          The eight CV outputs

 G1   G2    G3   G4                       output          The four gate/trigger outputs

 G2.1 G2.2 G2.3 G2.4        ...   G2.8    input/output    The eight gate jacks of the first G8 expander. Use G3.X, G4.X and G5.X for the 2nd , 3rd and 4th G8.

 G9 G10 G11 G12                           output          The four gate jacks of the X7 expander. These are always outputs.

 R1   R2    R3   R4                       output          The colored diagnostic LEDs on the back of the module

 R17 ... R48                              output          The colored LED squares on the ﬁrst, second, third and fourth G8 expander

 R49 ... R56                              output          The colored LED squares on the X7 expander




DROID manual for blue-6                                                                   52                                                                Table of contents at page 2
Registers on the controllers:

 Register                            Type      Description

 P1.1 P1.2 P2.1 P2.2 P3.1 P3.2 …     input     The pots on your P2B8, P4B2, P10 or M4 controllers. P3.2 is the 2nd pot on your 3rd controller.

 E1.1 E1.2 E1.3 E1.4 E2.1 …          special   The encoders of you E4 controllers. These registers can only be used in junction with the circuits encoder (see page
                                               189), encoderbank (see page 185) and encoquencer (see page 198).

 B1.1 B1.2 B2.1 … B2.1 B2.2 B2.3 …   input     The push buttons on your P2B8, P4B2 or B32 controllers. B3.6 is the 6th push button on your 3rd controller.

 L1.1 L1.2 L2.1 … L2.1 L2.2 L2.3 …   output    The LEDs in these push buttons

 R1.1    R1.2   R1.3    R1.4         output    The LEDs in the touch buttons of the M4 controller at position 1

5.14   More than one patch on the memory card


Sometimes you might want to have more than one                 The name of that ﬁle is DSTAXY .BIN, so for example
        patch on your card and switch back and forth be-       DSTA14.BIN if you load the patch droid14.ini. All you
tween these without going back to your computer. This          need to know is that each patch has it separate state. So
can be done if you have at least one controller with but-      if you e.g. have an algoquencer in each of two patches,
tons, such as P2B8, P4B2 or B32.                               it’s patterns will seperately loaded and saved.

It goes like this: Put your additional patches on the
card with special ﬁlenames in the format droidXY .ini,
where X is the number of the controller and Y the num-
ber of the button. Then for example droid14.ini will
be loaded if you first press and hold the button 4 on your
ﬁrst controller while then pressing the load button on the
master.

This way if you have one P2B8 you can choose between
nine different patches. If you have a second P2B8 con-
troller, this extends to 17 patches, because now holding
button 1 on controller 2 will load droid21.ini and so on.
A B32 gives you a total of 32 alternative patches to load
and so on. And yes: if you have 10 or more controllers
and some B32 amongst them, droid124.ini would be
loaded by button 24 on controller 1, but also by button
4 on controller 12.

Important: It is crucial that every of your patch ﬁles con-
tains the appropriate [p2b8] or other controller declara-
tions! Otherwise you won’t be able to switch over to the
other patches since button presses will not longer be reg-
istered by the          master. It will instead fall back to
the normal droid.ini in that case.

If you load a patch that way, the states of your circuits
are saved in a special ﬁle that accompanies the patch.


DROID manual for blue-6                                                                   58                               Table of contents at page 2
6   Controllers

6.1 Installing the controllers


Controllers are easy to install and use. The picture on the                                This is how a setup with two P2B8s on a master looks like:
right shows the back of the P2B8 controller, but the other
controllers look similar.
                                                              Jumper for terminating the
Each controller has two 6-pin connectors that are
                                                              chain
mounted in boxes (shrouded). They are labelled “LINK
OUT” (left) and “LINK IN” (right). These connectors are
for building a chain of controllers. Don’t mix this up with   Use these connectors.
the 6-pin header that is labelled “Debug”, which doesn’t
have a box!

With your controller you got a 6-pin ribbon cable. Con-
nect one end of it to the shrouded 6-pin controller con-
nector of your master and the other end to the “LINK IN”
of your ﬁrst controller.
                                                              Don’t use this one!
Take another 6-pin cable and wire the “LINK OUT” of your
ﬁrst controller to the “LINK IN” of your second controller.
Continue until all controllers are chained together.

Finally: Every controller also has a three-pin header with
the labels “LAST” and “PARK”. When you get the mod-
ule there is a small connector (“jumper”) between the two
pins that are labelled “LAST”. This jumper is crucial for
making the chain work. Here is the rule:                                                   If you switch on your system after connecting the con-
                                                                                           trollers, those with LEDs should make a short power up
    • On the last controller, the jumper must be in the
                                                                                           animation. This does not mean that they are wired and
      position “LAST”.
                                                                                           jumpered correctly, though. To make a real test, you need
    • On all other controllers, the jumper must be in the
                                                                                           to prepare a         patch, as you will see below.
      “PARK” position or removed (The park position is
      just for your convencience that you don’t lose the                                   Note: The M4 controller (see page 71) needs an addi-
      jumper).                                                                             tional power connector to your Eurorack system. The
                                                                                           other controllers are powered by the master.

                                                                match the order of your controllers in the chain, begin-
                                                                ning with the one that is directly connected to the mas-
                                                                ter. Here is an example with two P2B8s followed by one

                                                                [p2b8]

6.6 The P10 controller


                          The P10 controller has two big pots (the same as the P2B8    P1.1, P1.2, P1.3 ... P1.10.
                          controller) and eight small pots. That makes a total of 10
                          pots, which are all behaving in the same way. They are       The P10 is handy if you need to control lots of continuous
                          numbered from 1 to 10, so if your P10 would be the ﬁrst      values. The small pots are not as easy to operate as the
                          in the chain, these pots are adressed in a       patch by    big ones but they are very space efﬁcient.

7   The G8 expander

7.1 Introduction                                              7.2 Installation                                               The G8 version 2 has two connectors. Here use the right
                                                                                                                             one labelled “Master”:
                The G8 expander gives you eight addi-         If you have just one G8 version 1, simply use the 8 pin rib-
                tional jacks, each of which can be used       bon cable that has been shipped with your G8 and con-
                as a gate or trigger input or output.         nect the G8 to the 8 pin port of the master as shown in the
                They are ideal for working with clocks,       following picture. Put the red stripe down in both mod-
                gates and triggers, but can be used for       ules.
                simple CV modulations, as well. There

                are two hardware versions of the G8.
                Version 2 was introduced 2023 and al-
                lows you to chain up to four G8 ex-
                panders to one master. For that pur-
                pose it has two connectors on the back:
                one to be connected to the master, one
                for the next G8.

                The original G8 version 1 has only one
                connector. There is no need to be sad
                if your G8 is version 1, since it still can
                work in a chain with more G8s if it is the
                last one. So if you want a second G8,
                simply get a version 2 one and use the
                old one as the second G8.

8.3 Installation                                                                                                                If you don’t like the Forge, you can edit droid.ini di-

One key feature of the X7 is working with MIDI. The com-

verter in Eurorack land. Here are some of the key fea-                                                                       cables and always sends on cable 0. Future software up-

The LEDs                                                                                                                 trollers and so on. This X7 can give you access to the vast

When working with MIDI, watch the corresponding LEDs.                                                                    The hardware connection is done either with the 3.5 mm
Here is what the colors mean:                                                                                            TRS jack or via USB (or both at the same time). The X7
                                                                                                                         comes with two identical TRS ↔ DIN adapters, so you can
 black        no data transmitted                                                                                        use the much more wide spread classical MIDI cables with
 dim white    steady activity                                                                                            DIN plugs.
                                                           The fourth LED shows MIDI data via outgoing TRS:
 green        note on                                                                                                    Even if you don’t use our adapters but use the 3.5 mm
                                                                                                                         jacks directly, you don’t need to care about MIDI “A and
 red          note off
                                                                                                                         B”. The X7 does autosensing at its input. Either way will
 blue         some other MIDI event                                                                                      work. Just make sure you use stereo cables. Normal mod-
                                                                                                                         ular patch cables don’t work.
The top right LED shows the status of USB-MIDI:
                                                                                                                         The basic operation is super simple. All is done with the
                                                                                                                         circuit midiin (see page 260). This example converts
                                                                                                                         MIDI to a pitch CV at output O1 and a gate at output O2:


                                                                                                                         [midiin]
                                                                                                                             pitch = O1
                                                                                                                             gate = O2

                                                           MIDI to CV (MIDI input)
                                                                                                                         The source is the TRS jack. But you can easily select MIDI
                                                                                                                         via USB instead with the usb parameter:
                                                           The most common application for MIDI and modular syn-
                                                           thesizers is converting MIDI note events to CV/gate sig-
                                                           nals. When you press a key on a MIDI keyboard or when         [midiin]
The third LED shows MIDI data via incoming TRS:
                                                           a MIDI sequencer starts playing a note, a MIDI “note on”          usb = 1
                                                           message is being sent over the wire. Likewise at the end          pitch = O1
                                                           of the note a “note off” message is sent.                         gate = O2

                                                           A typical MIDI to CV module receives these messages and
                                                                                                                         Per default, midiin processes notes from all 16 MIDI
                                                           feeds at least two jacks: one with the pitch of the cur-
                                                                                                                         channels. You can select one speciﬁc channel with the
                                                           rently played note in form of the typical 1 volt per octave
                                                                                                                         channel jack:

Note: For this all to work it is very important that your

use German umlauts or any other special character that’s
not part of the English language (you would do that just
in comments anyway).

8.10   Software update for the X7                            X7 upgrade with the MASTER


Unlike the simple expanders like the P2B8 or the P10, the    Here are the next steps for an X7 ﬁrmware upgrade if you
X7 has a rather sophisticated software. Some bugs might      have an MASTER:
be found. And new feature ideas will be implemented. So                                                                   In case of an error, all 16 LEDs blink in one color. If all
                                                                1. Bring the master into the maintenance mode (see
The X7 has a software update procedure.                                                                                   LEDs blink yellow, the ﬁrmware ﬁle is missing (which is


DROID manual for blue-6                                                                 85                                                               Table of contents at page 2
strange, because it was there at the beginning):                                                                          nostic LEDs on the back of the module, you will see a
                                                                                                                          pulsating white “dot” moving in circles through the four
                                                                                                                          LEDs. This indicates that the master is ready for the up-
                                                                                                                          date. Also the LED in the button blinks slowly (once a sec-
                                                                                                                          ond).

                                                                                                                          As soon as the X7 is attached (which might be immedi-
                                                                                                                          ately if it was already attached when you powered up),
                                                                                                                          the X7 fetches the ﬁrmware ﬁle and loads it into its inter-
                                                                                                                          nal ﬂash memory. While this is ongoing, the button LED
                                                                                                                          is lit permanently.

                                                              After the upgrade, you need to leave the maintenance        If everything goes well, the four diagnostic LEDs on the
                                                              menu on your master. Do this by navigating the blink-       MASTER18 show a green “progress bar” and the eight
All blinking blue means an invalid size of the ﬁrmware ﬁle:   ing cursor to the white LED 1 and press the button a bit    LEDs on the X7 do the same with the same color. Then
                                                              longer:                                                     the X7 restarts and does it’s usual small LED start anima-
                                                                                                                          tion and should display the new version number.

                                                                                                                          Now the button of the master ﬂashes fast. You can now
                                                                                                                          press the button to repeat the upgrade procedure or up-
                                                                                                                          date another X7.

                                                                                                                          Note: When you are ﬁnished with the update, you need
                                                                                                                          to remove the ﬁle x7.fw from the master’s SD card.
                                                                                                                          Otherwise the master will enter the procedure again
                                                                                                                          and again!

                                                                                                                          Hint: if enter USB-Stick mode, the upgrade procedure is

ning at approximately 5.5 kHz! In each cycle of the loop

tasks. This mode is a bit “hidden” so that you do not en-   release the button, the          enters the maintenance
ter it accidentally. You enter the maintenance mode by      mode. If you let go the button before this you go back
holding the button on the master for a couple of seconds.   into normal operation.

light blue LEDs going from O8 over to I1 starts:            In maintenance mode you will see a white “cursor” blink-
                                                            ing at the LED for I1. Cell I3 is red, Cell I4 is magenta:

                                                                                                                         ibration mode):




When the blue LEDs reach I1, continue holding the but-
ton. DROID restarts. Still hold the button. Now the ani-
mation of the blue LEDs starts in the opposite direction:   The four positions I1 ... I4 represent four different menu
                                                            options:

                                                                  restart the        .

                                                                  keep calibration).

15 Musical scales

Here your ﬁnd all possible 108 values that you can use in the degree input of various circuits like minifonion (see page 279) or chord (see page 154). This table might indeed look
strange. The reason is that it reﬂects the internal scale structure of the Sinfonion. Every 12 scales refer to one mode of the Sinfonion. This is also the reason why some scale appear
twice. All scales are noted in the assumption that root is set to 0, which is a C.

DROID manual for blue-6                                                                             109           Table of contents at page 2
16 Reference of all circuits

This is a reference of all circuits that are supported by ﬁrmware version blue-6of        . The description of each circuit is made of two parts: a general introduction with some examples
and a table of all input and output jacks that the circuit offers.

Just like real synth modules the input and output jacks of          ’s circuits have different characteristics, which are denoted by one of seven symbols in the reference:

            Jacks with the symbol          work with continous CVs in the full voltage range from -10 V to +10 V.
      1V
      Oct   This symbol denotes jacks that work on a precise “one volt per octave” base. Such outputs can be patched to the V/Oct inputs of VCOs. Inputs with this symbol expect pitch
            information e.g. from sequencers or musical quantizers.

  0     1   This jack has a range from 0.0 to 1.0. Input values greater than 1.0 are truncated to 1.0, values below zero are set to 0.0. This input can be seen as a fraction or percentage.
            When you use ﬁxed values you can write percentages, for example 55% instead of 0.55. Since potentiometers yield values in exactly that range you can directly assign one
            to such a CV. If you control that CV with an external voltage, the range is 0 V … 10 V.

  0 0.5 1   This jack is very similar to that of type 0    1 , but its neutral value is in the middle position – at 0.5 or 50% or 5 V. An example is the jack distribution of the algoquencer

            circuit: At the middle position beats are distributed evenly in the bar. Left or right of the center the beats are more oriented to the ﬁrst or second half of the bar, respectively.
            If you assign a pot, the center position of the pot is the neutral position.Values out of the range 0.0 … 1.0 are truncated into that range. Hint: The input notch of the pot
            circuit at page 329 helps you exactly centering a pot at 0.5. The range for external voltages is 0 V ... 10 V.
  1 2 3     This jack operates with integer numbers such as 1, 2, 3 and so on. An example is the length input of the euklid circuit. For some jacks 0 can be allowed as well. One example
            is the inputoffset jack of the switch circuit. Any non-integer number will be rounded to the nearest integer. So a value of 0.6 will be interpreted as 1. Wiring an external
            input directly to such a jack does not make much sense, since the range 0 V ... 10 V just maps to 0 ... 1. For a 2 you would need 20 V. So you need to add some scaling, for
            example somejack = I1 * 10, which converts an external 2 V to the number 2.

            This denotes a stepped voltage. This is one that only appears in discrete steps. An example of a stepped output CV is the pitch output of the sequencer circuit.

            Jacks with this symbol just know 0 and 1 or on and off. These are things like a gate from an envelope, where the length of the input counts. Some circuits also have switch
            inputs or settings of that type that enable features like “looping on”. Also all inputs that are meant to be wired to buttons like B1.1 are of that type, since buttons output
            exactly such gate signals. Output jacks of that type always either send 0.0 (0 V) or 1.0 (10 V). Using G1 … G8 for these is also ﬁne, but they output 5 V instead of 10 V. When
            you you wire an external input to such a jack, it will see a 1 at a voltage of at least 1 V and and 0 otherwise.

            These jacks are trigger inputs or outputs. A trigger input just is interested about points in time where the voltage changes from 0 to some positive value above roughly 1 V.
            The duration of the time where the voltage is not zero is not interesting here. A typical use are clock or reset inputs. When the          outputs a trigger, is it sends a signal
            of 10 V for a duration of 10 ms. Using G1 … G8 from the G8 expander for these is just ﬁne, but the output voltage will be 5 V in that case. For external input voltages use any
            regular clock/trigger/gate signal from your system.

The column Default shows the value a parameter has if you don’t patch anything into it. Here the special symbol + denotes a certain “intelligent” behaviour when this jack is not
used. Please read the description for details.




DROID manual for blue-6                                                                      110                                                                  Table of contents at page 2
Memory consumption


Nothing in the world is for free. And also using circuits has a price: memory. Every circuit you use need its share of RAM. Your      has about 110.000 bytes of RAM free to be used
by circuits. Every circuit needs a certain amount of RAM – plus some extra bytes for every used parameter.

The following table shows the RAM usage of each of the circuits:

adc                                                   56       faderbank                                             616       octave                                            32
algoquencer                                          880       fadermatrix                                           640       once                                              24
arpeggio                                             144       firefacecontrol                                      1088       outputcalibrator                                  40
bernoulli                                             32       flipflop                                               40       polytool                                         240
burst                                                 40       fold                                                   32       pot                                              120
button                                                96       fourstatebutton                                        40       quantizer                                         48
buttongroup                                          440       gatetool                                               56       queue                                            312
calibrator                                           224       ifequal                                                32       random                                            32
case                                                  88       lfo                                                   216       recorder                                        1712
chord                                                136       logic                                                  56       sample                                            40
clocktool                                             96       math                                                   64       select                                            24
compare                                               32       matrixmixer                                           176       sequencer                                        168
contour                                              112       midifileplayer                                       6384       sinfonionlink                                     56
copy                                                  24       midiin                                                560       slew                                              48
crossfader                                            40       midiout                                               664       spring                                            56
cvlooper                                           17336       midithrough                                           240       superjust                                         64
dac                                                   56       minifonion                                            112       switch                                           104
delay                                               1672       mixer                                                  48       switchedpot                                       88
detune                                                56       motoquencer                                          1168       timing                                            56
droid                                                 72       motorfader                                            112       togglebutton                                      48
encoder                                              184       multicompare                                           56       transient                                         56
encoderbank                                          736       noop                                                   16       triggerdelay                                     248
encoquencer                                         1336       notchedpot                                             40       unusedfaders                                      32
euklid                                                48       notebuttons                                           128
explin                                                32       nudge                                                 144


In addition each used input or output parameter need some memory, depending on its type:

    • Normal inputs need 12 bytes.
    • Trigger inputs need 16 bytes.
    • Tap tempo inputs need 30 bytes.


DROID manual for blue-6                                                                   111                                                             Table of contents at page 2
    • Normal outputs need 4 bytes.
    • Trigger outputs need 8 bytes.

In addition each internal patch cable and each unique constant (like 1.5 or -12) needs 8 bytes. Note: If you are using the Droid Forge for creating your patches, you don’t need to do
any computations yourself. The Forge will always show you the exact memory consumption of your patch.




DROID manual for blue-6                                                                  112                                                              Table of contents at page 2
16.1    adc – AD Converter with 12 bits


This circuit converts an input value into                   [adc]
a binary representation of up to 12 bits.                       input = I1
Consider the following example:                                 minimum = 0.1 # 1V
                                                                maximum = 0.5 # 4V
                                                                bit1 = O1
[adc]                                                           bit2 = O2
    input = I1                                                  bit3 = O3
    bit1 = O1
    bit2 = O2
    bit3 = O3                                               Now the table looks like this:

                                                                 input       bit1    bit2     bit3    bit value
In this example three bits are being used.
                                                              −∞ ... 0.15      0       0        0         0
Three bits can represent a number from 0
to 7. These are mapped to the input range from 0 to 1 (or    0.15 ... 0.20     0       0        1         1
0 V to 10 V) in the following way:                           0.20 ... 0.25     0       1        0         2
                                                             0.25 ... 0.30     0       1        1         3
       input       bit1    bit2    bit3     bit value
                                                             0.30 ... 0.35     1       0        0         4
  −∞ ... 0.125       0       0       0         0
                                                             0.35 ... 0.40     1       0        1         5
 0.125 ... 0.250     0       0       1         1
                                                             0.40 ... 0.45     1       1        0         6
 0.250 ... 0.375     0       1       0         2
                                                              0.45 ... ∞       1       1        1         7
 0.375 ... 0.500     0       1       1         3
 0.500 ... 0.625     1       0       0         4            If you use more of the bit-outputs you get more resolu-
 0.625 ... 0.750     1       0       1         5            tion. For example if you use bit1 ... bit8, the total range
                                                            will be divided into 256 equal pieces. Since bit 1 is the
 0.750 ... 0.875     1       1       0         6
                                                            most signiﬁcant bit, adding more and more bits will not
   0.875 ... ∞       1       1       1         7
                                                            change the way bit 1 is behaving.

DROID manual for blue-6                                                                113                                Table of contents at page 2
 Input                    Type   Default   Description

 input (i)                       0.0       Input signal to convert to binary representation.
 minimum (m)                     0.0       The lowest assumed input value. This value and all lower values will be converted to the bit sequence 000000000000.

 maximum (x)                     1.0       The highest assumed input value. This value and all higher values will be converted to the bit sequence 111111111111.



 Output                   Type             Description

 bit1 ... bit12 (b)                        The 12 bit outputs. bit1 is the MSB – the most signiﬁcant bit. The LSB (least signiﬁcant bit) is the highest output that
                                           you actually patch. If you do not need the full resolution of 12 bits, simply just use the ﬁrst couple of outputs.
